era_context: The early 14th century: the Yuan Dynasty in China is experiencing increasing instability; the Genkō Rebellion in Japan begins; the Hundred Years' War between England and France is in its early phase; the Ottoman Empire is consolidating power in Anatolia; the Avignon Papacy continues; the Mali Empire is in decline in West Africa.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica and South America (post-conquest sources only)
Central Asia and the Steppe (limited written records)
Southeast Asia (limited written records in European sources)

events.title: Genkō Rebellion begins in Japan
events.region: Japan, primarily Kyoto and surrounding regions
events.description: The Genkō Rebellion (1331–1333) began in 1331 when Emperor Go-Daigo, with support from samurai including Ashikaga Takauji and Nitta Yoshisada, rose against the Hōjō Regency. The rebellion was motivated by Go-Daigo's desire to restore direct imperial rule and end the shogunate system. The initial phase of the rebellion saw mixed results, with Go-Daigo being captured and exiled to the Oki Islands. However, the rebellion would ultimately succeed in 1333 with the fall of the Hōjō and the establishment of the Kenmu Restoration. The year 1331 marks the beginning of this transformative conflict.

sources.name: Taiheiki (Chronicle of the Great Peace), compiled 14th century
Genkō Heiki (Records of the Genkō Rebellion), contemporary or near-contemporary

events.certainty_note: The Genkō Rebellion is well-documented in Japanese chronicles. The 1331 start date is secure.
cross_references: Led to 1333-001 (Fall of Kamakura and Hōjō)

events.title: Famine in China
events.description: The Yuan Dynasty experienced a severe famine in 1331, likely related to climatic stress, poor harvests, and the disruption caused by the Genkō Rebellion in Japan and other regional conflicts. Chinese sources record widespread crop failures and food shortages. The famine contributed to social unrest and peasant rebellions that would destabilize Yuan rule throughout the 1330s–1360s. The exact extent and regional distribution of the famine are difficult to determine from surviving sources.

sources.name: Yuan Shi (History of the Yuan Dynasty), compiled 14th century

events.certainty_note: Chinese sources record famines in the early 1330s, but precise dating to 1331 is uncertain. The famine is attested in later compilations but not in contemporary primary sources.

events.title: Hundred Years' War tensions escalate
events.description: In 1331, tensions between England and France escalated as Edward III of England continued to assert claims to the French throne and English forces engaged in military operations in Aquitaine and other disputed territories. While the formal declaration of war came in 1337, the military and diplomatic conflicts of 1331 represent important precursors. The disputes over succession to the French throne and control of English-held territories in France were the underlying causes of the Hundred Years' War.

sources.name: English royal records and diplomatic correspondence

events.certainty_note: English-French tensions in the early 1330s are documented in royal records and later chronicles. The year 1331 is approximate; the formal declaration of war came in 1337.
cross_references: Led to 1337-XXX (Formal declaration of Hundred Years' War)

events.title: Ottoman Empire consolidates power in Anatolia
events.description: In 1331, the Ottoman Empire, under Osman I and his successor Orhan I, continued to consolidate power in Anatolia, expanding territory at the expense of the declining Byzantine Empire and rival Turkish beyliks. The Ottomans captured the city of Bursa in 1326 and continued to expand their domain throughout the 1330s. The year 1331 represents part of this broader process of Ottoman expansion and consolidation that would eventually lead to the establishment of a major imperial power.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in the 1330s is documented in Byzantine sources and later Ottoman chronicles. The year 1331 is assigned by convention; specific events in this year are not well-documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The Genkō Rebellion is traditionally dated 1331–1333, with 1331 as the start date. However, some modern Japanese historians have proposed slightly different chronologies based on reexamination of sources. The famine in China in 1331 is attested in later sources but not in contemporary primary documents; the exact year is uncertain. Ottoman expansion in 1331 is not specifically documented; the year is assigned by convention based on broader patterns of Ottoman growth in the 1330s.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1331 is sparse. Japanese events (Genkō Rebellion) are well-recorded in contemporary and near-contemporary chronicles. English-French tensions are documented in royal records and later chronicles. Chinese and Ottoman events rely on later compilations and are difficult to date precisely. The year falls in a period of significant geopolitical change across multiple regions, but surviving sources are unevenly distributed. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Central Asia are absent from surviving written records.

graph_edges.note: The Genkō Rebellion that began in 1331 continued through 1332 and culminated in 1333
The 1331 famine in China contributed to peasant unrest and rebellions in subsequent years
English-French tensions escalated into the Second War of Scottish Independence and broader conflicts
